title: Highly Irregular Microstructures and TN Configurations
speaker: John Ball
abstract: Remarkable martensitic microstructures are observed in the alloy  Ti76Nb22Al2 , which undergoes a cubic to orthorhombic transformation with six martensitic variants Ui=UTi greater than 0 having middle eigenvalue λ2(Ui) very close to 1. Assuming that λ2(Ui)=1 there are exactly 12 matrices in the set of energy wells ⋃6i=1SO(3)Ui that are rank-one connected to 1. This set of 12 matrices has no rank-one connections.  We attempt to understand the observed microstructures by studying gradient Young measures, exact gradients and TN-configurations supported on these 12 matrices. This is joint work with Tomonari Inamura and Francesco Della Porta.

proof_stages.label: Experimental target: a twelve-color martensitic microstructure
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from Inamura's Ti-Nb-Al experiment, explaining how a single grain is imaged and motivating the claim that the observed colors approximate twelve deformation-gradient values with no mutual rank-one compatibility.

proof_stages.label: Nonlinear-elastic model and crystallographic energy wells
proof_stages.summary: The alloy is modeled by nonlinear elasticity with frame indifference and cubic symmetry, producing an austenite well and six orthorhombic martensite wells whose stretch matrices have middle eigenvalue essentially one and often determinant one.

proof_stages.label: Austenite-martensite rank-one connections and incompatible twelve-point set
proof_stages.summary: Using the Hadamard jump condition, the middle-eigenvalue-one condition yields exactly two rank-one connections from the identity to each of the six martensite wells, giving twelve matrices whose average contains the identity when alpha beta equals one but which are pairwise rank-one incompatible.

proof_stages.label: Relaxation framework: convex hulls, quasiconvexity, and Young measures
proof_stages.summary: The analysis is placed in the standard calculus-of-variations framework of convex, polyconvex, quasiconvex, and rank-one convex hulls together with homogeneous gradient Young measures and laminates.

proof_stages.label: TN and degenerate TN configurations as laminate mechanisms
proof_stages.summary: TN configurations and their degenerate versions are introduced as combinatorial rank-one constructions that place line segments and probability measures supported on finitely many matrices inside the rank-one convex hull.

proof_stages.label: Degenerate T12/T8 structures for the martensitic twelve-point set
proof_stages.summary: Applying the TN formalism to the twelve matrices shows there are no genuine TN configurations, but when alpha beta equals one there is a degenerate T12 configuration, a laminate of equal weights, and in fact degenerate T8 configurations obtained from the nullspace computation.

proof_stages.label: Exact-gradient question and Dirichlet rigidity obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The speaker distinguishes approximate gradient Young-measure microstructures from exact solutions and proves a partial rigidity theorem, via bounded-distortion openness, ruling out an exact gradient with identity boundary data and gradients in the twelve-point set.

proof_stages.label: Quasiconvex-hull conjectures, stress, convex integration, and dynamics
proof_stages.summary: The final part formulates the open problem of determining the quasiconvex hull, notes stressed near-well interfaces and convex-integration approximations, and discusses dynamic and probabilistic mechanisms behind the observed large, power-law-distributed microstructure.

examples.name: Nickel-aluminum layered martensite
examples.note: The nickel-aluminum picture is used as a contrasting, more typical layered martensitic microstructure, illustrating what happens when compatibility is achieved by laminates rather than by the twelve incompatible variants seen in the main alloy.
examples.engagement_reason: the nickel-aluminum layered martensite is only cited as a more common contrasting layered microstructure, without analyzing or computing anything on it

passes.reason: A nickel-aluminum layered martensite is presented as a common laminate microstructure used for comparison, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Preiss five-matrix exact-gradient example
examples.note: The famous two-dimensional example with five pairwise rank-one-incompatible matrices illustrates why one cannot dismiss the possibility of an exact gradient merely from the absence of rank-one interfaces.
examples.engagement_reason: the Preiss five-matrix example is invoked as a known 2x2 exact-gradient example with no rank-one connections, but no construction or verification is carried out

passes.reason: Preiss’s five incompatible matrices give an exact gradient, countering the naive impossibility from no rank-one connections, and five is noted as the minimal number.
Preiss's five-matrix construction is the minimal-size exact-gradient example with no rank-one connections and refutes the naive obstruction from pairwise incompatibility.
Preiss's five-matrix construction is the minimal-size exact-gradient phenomenon and refutes the naive expectation that pairwise incompatibility forbids such gradients
Preiss’s five-matrix construction is the minimal-size exact-gradient example with no rank-one connections, refuting the naive obstruction from pairwise incompatibility.
the five-matrix construction is the minimal-support exact-gradient example showing that pairwise incompatibility need not preclude an exact gradient
minimal five-matrix construction shows no rank-one connections need not forbid a nontrivial exact gradient
Preiss's five-matrix construction shows exact gradients can exist with no rank-one connections, and five is noted as the minimal possible number
Preiss's five-matrix construction refutes the naive rigidity claim that no rank-one connections forbid exact gradients, and five is noted as the minimal possible number.
the minimal five-matrix construction refutes the expectation that no rank-one connections preclude an exact gradient
Preiss's five-matrix construction refutes the naive rigidity from having no rank-one connections and is minimal in the number of wells
Preiss’s five incompatible 2x2 matrices refute the naive no-rank-one-no-exact-gradient expectation, with five noted as minimal
refutes naive rigidity from absence of rank-one connections, and five matrices is the minimal such exact-gradient construction

examples.name: Random plate-nucleation model with two or four directions
examples.note: The stochastic construction of horizontal/vertical, then four-direction, plates illustrates a possible probabilistic ingredient in martensitic pattern formation and in the observed power-law distribution of plate lengths.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly runs through the random plate-nucleation process, choosing random points and horizontal/vertical directions, stopping plates at previous ones, and comparing two- and four-direction simulations

passes.reason: The two- or four-direction random plate-nucleation process is a simplified illustrative stochastic model for similar microstructures and power laws.
The two- or four-direction nucleation process is a reduced toy/minimal-direction model for the fuller twelve-direction martensitic setting.
the two- or four-direction random nucleation process is a simplified illustrative stochastic model rather than an extreme or adversarial case

examples.name: Shape-memory bending and cofactor-condition alloys
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker describes bending a martensitic shape-memory alloy and explains that specially tuned lattice parameters give extra microstructures and hence greater reversible flexibility.
examples.engagement_reason: shape-memory bending and cofactor-condition alloys are described generally as motivation and known behavior, including hysteresis reduction, but not worked through concretely

passes.reason: Cofactor-condition shape-memory alloys are engineered at exact compatibility conditions giving unusually low hysteresis and high flexibility, but are used as an application rather than a counterexample.
Cofactor-condition alloys are finely tuned critical-compatibility cases engineered to give extra microstructures and very small hysteresis.
